title: Stockton North

infobox.value: is a [constituency](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_Parliament_constituencies "List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies")[[n 1]](#cite_note-1) covering the town of [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees") in County Durham and other nearby settlements in the [Borough of Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees") located north of the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees"), represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [UK Parliament](/wiki/UK_Parliament "UK Parliament") by [Chris McDonald](/wiki/Chris_McDonald_(politician) "Chris McDonald (politician)"), a member of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), having been elected in the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").

The Borough of Stockton-on-Tees wards of Blue Hall, Charltons, Elm Tree, Glebe, Grange, Hardwick, Marsh House, Mile House, Newtown, Northfield, Norton, Portrack and Tilery, Roseworth, St Aidan's, St Cuthbert's, Whitton, and Wolviston.

The Borough of Stockton-on-Tees wards of Blue Hall, Charltons, Glebe, Grange, Hardwick, Marsh House, Mile House, Newtown, Northfield, Norton, Portrack and Tilery, Roseworth, St Aidan's, St Cuthbert's, Whitton, and Wolviston.

The Borough of Stockton-on-Tees wards of Billingham Central, Billingham East, Billingham North, Billingham South, Billingham West, Hardwick, Newtown, Northern Parishes, Norton North, Norton South, Norton West, Roseworth, Stockton Town Centre, and Western Parishes.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Parliamentary constituency in the United Kingdom, 1983 onwards

| Stockton North | |

**Stockton North** is a [constituency](/wiki/List_of_United_Kingdom_Parliament_constituencies "List of United Kingdom Parliament constituencies")[[n 1]](#cite_note-1) covering the town of [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees") in County Durham and other nearby settlements in the [Borough of Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees") located north of the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees"), represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [UK Parliament](/wiki/UK_Parliament "UK Parliament") by [Chris McDonald](/wiki/Chris_McDonald_(politician) "Chris McDonald (politician)"), a member of the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"), having been elected in the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election").

## Boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stockton_North&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Boundaries")]

![Map](https://maps.wikimedia.org/img/osm-intl,a,a,a,300x200.png?lang=en&domain=en.wikipedia.org&title=Stockton_North&revid=1344245767&groups=_396c115094193acf6db22d7aa8ee50ff43990367&parser=legacy)

Map of 2010–2024 boundaries

### Historic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stockton_North&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Historic")]

**1983–1997**: The Borough of Stockton-on-Tees wards of Blue Hall, Charltons, Elm Tree, Glebe, Grange, Hardwick, Marsh House, Mile House, Newtown, Northfield, Norton, Portrack and Tilery, Roseworth, St Aidan's, St Cuthbert's, Whitton, and Wolviston.

**1997–2010**: The Borough of Stockton-on-Tees wards of Blue Hall, Charltons, Glebe, Grange, Hardwick, Marsh House, Mile House, Newtown, Northfield, Norton, Portrack and Tilery, Roseworth, St Aidan's, St Cuthbert's, Whitton, and Wolviston.

**2010–2024**: The Borough of Stockton-on-Tees wards of Billingham Central, Billingham East, Billingham North, Billingham South, Billingham West, Hardwick, Newtown, Northern Parishes, Norton North, Norton South, Norton West, Roseworth, Stockton Town Centre, and Western Parishes.

### Current

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stockton_North&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Current")]

Further to the [2023 periodic review of Westminster constituencies](/wiki/2023_periodic_review_of_Westminster_constituencies "2023 periodic review of Westminster constituencies"), which came into effect for the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), the composition of the constituency was defined as comprising the following wards of the Borough of Stockton-on-Tees (as they existed on 1 December 2020):

* Billingham Central; Billingham East; Billingham North; Billingham South; Billingham West; Hardwick and Salters Lane; Newtown; Northern Parishes; Norton North; Norton South; Norton West; Parkfield and Oxbridge; Roseworth; Stockton Town Centre.[[1]](#cite_note-2)

*In order to bring the electorate within the permitted electoral range, the Parkfield and Oxbridge ward was transferred in from [Stockton South](/wiki/Stockton_South "Stockton South") (renamed [Stockton West](/wiki/Stockton_West "Stockton West")) in exchange for Western Parishes.*

Following a local government boundary review which came into effect in May 2023,[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-4) the constituency now comprises the following wards of the Borough of Stockton-on-Tees from the 2024 general election:

* Billingham Central; Billingham East; Billingham North; Billingham South; Billingham West & Wolviston; Eaglescliffe East (small part); Grangefield (majority); Hardwick & Salters Lane; Newtown; Northern Parishes (majority); Norton Central; Norton North; Norton South; Ropner; Roseworth; Stockton Town Centre; and a very small part of Hartburn.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

Stockton North consists of the northern part of the [Borough of Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Borough_of_Stockton-on-Tees "Borough of Stockton-on-Tees") in County Durham, including the [town centre](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees") and the nearby towns and villages of [Billingham](/wiki/Billingham "Billingham"), [Norton](/wiki/Norton,_County_Durham "Norton, County Durham"), [Wolviston](/wiki/Wolviston "Wolviston") and [Thorpe Thewles](/wiki/Thorpe_Thewles "Thorpe Thewles").[[5]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stockton_North&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

The constituency was created for the [1983 general election](/wiki/1983_United_Kingdom_general_election "1983 United Kingdom general election"), partially replacing the former [Stockton-on-Tees constituency](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Stockton-on-Tees (UK Parliament constituency)"). The outgoing MP for Stockton-on-Tees was [Bill Rodgers](/wiki/Bill_Rodgers,_Baron_Rodgers_of_Quarry_Bank "Bill Rodgers, Baron Rodgers of Quarry Bank"), who had held the seat since 1962. He had been a [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") member until 1981, when he left to found the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(UK) "Social Democratic Party (UK)") (SDP).

The 1983 election was the first since Rodgers had left the Labour Party, and he was narrowly defeated by Labour's [Frank Cook](/wiki/Frank_Cook_(politician) "Frank Cook (politician)"). Cook held the seat with majorities between 16% and 48% until the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), when after 27 years as the MP he was de-selected by his local party. Cook chose to run again however, as an [independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") candidate. Cook polled less than 5% of the vote, fifth of the seven candidates who stood, and joined four of these in forfeiting his [deposit](/wiki/Deposit_(politics) "Deposit (politics)") and the seat was held by the Labour Party's next candidate, [Alex Cunningham](/wiki/Alex_Cunningham "Alex Cunningham").

In November 2021, Cunningham announced his intention to stand down at the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"),[[6]](#cite_note-7) when the seat was won by his successor as Labour candidate, [Chris McDonald](/wiki/Chris_McDonald_(politician) "Chris McDonald (politician)").

## Constituency profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stockton_North&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Constituency profile")]

The town of [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees") is a significant exports manufacturing and processing base in the United Kingdom. Stockton North has often in economically troubled times significantly more unemployment than Stockton South: workless claimants, registered jobseekers, were in November 2012 significantly above the national average of 3.8%, at 7.0% of the population based on a statistical compilation by *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* compared to 4.5% in Stockton South.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

In November 2023 Home Secretary [James Cleverly](/wiki/James_Cleverly "James Cleverly") was accused of calling the constituency "a shithole" during a Commons debate. The comment was said to have been made after the prime minister [Rishi Sunak](/wiki/Rishi_Sunak "Rishi Sunak") was challenged by MP [Alex Cunningham](/wiki/Alex_Cunningham "Alex Cunningham") on the level of child poverty in his constituency.[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10)
